JUST  PUBLISHED— SEVENTH  EDITION. 

THE  HISTORY  OF  ROMANISM, 

FROM  ITS  EARLIEST  ORIGIN  TO  THE  PRESENT  TIME, 

BY    THE 

REV.  JOHN  DOWLING,  A.  M. 

Complete  in  one  splendid  octavo  volume  of  nearly  700  pages,  printed  in  large  type, 
and  embellished  with  more  than 

FIFTY  HIGHLY  FINISHED  ENGRAVINGS, 

Chiefly  from  Original  Designs. 


Price  Three  Dollars. 


THE  PUBLISHER  TO  THE   READER. 

In  presenting  the  following  extracts  from  a  few  of  the  flattering  reviews  and 
notices  of  this  "  History  of  Romanism,"  the  publisher  would  return  his  gratefui 
acknowledgments  not  only  to  a  discerning  public  who  have  received  the  work  with 
so  much  enthusiasm,  as  entirely  to  exhaust  six  editions  of  one  thousand  copies 
each,  in  the  first  three  months  from  its  publication,  but  also  to  the  numerous  and  warm- 
hearted friends  of  Protestantism  in  the  editorial  and  clerical  ranks,  who  have  for- 
warded to  him  the  most  flattering  commendations  of  the  learning,  ability  and  re- 
search exhibited  by  the  author  in  the  preparation  of  the  work,  and  of  the  style  of 
printing,  embellishment  and  binding  in  which  the  publisher  has  issued  it  from  the 
press. 

Sustained  by  the  expressed  opinions  of  so  many  editors  and  learned  divines  of 
all  Protestant  denominations  that  the  work  is  destined  to  no  ephemeral  existence, 
and  that  it  will  doubtless  become  A  STANDARD  WORK,  essential  to  the  library 
of  every  Protestant  clergyman  and  well-informed  layman — the  publisher  has  stereo- 
typed the  work  and  made  extensive  arrangements  for  the  supply  of  Agents  and 
Booksellers  in  any  quantities.     The  Seventh  Edition  is  now  ready  for  delivery. 

In  compiling  the  following  list  of  selections  from  the  large  number  of  reviews 
and  testimonials  which  have  been  received,  the  publisher  has  been  governed  rather 

by  a  desire  to  introduce 

THE    OPINIONS    OF  ALL   THE    LEADING    DENOMINATIONS 
OF  PROTESTANT  CHRISTIANS, 

than  by  a  wish  to  select  the  most  ilattering  and  commendatory,  from  any  one  particular 
denomination.  A  few  have  been  added  from  some  of  our  most  ably  conducted 
literary  or  secular  periodicals,  connected  with  no  particular  religious  sect. 

The  letter  from  the  Rev.  Dr.  Giustiniani,  the  converted  Roman  Catholic  priest, 
is  of  especial  value,  coining  as  it  does,  from  one  born  and  educated  in  the  city  of 
Rome,  and  abundantly  qualified  by  education,  observation  and  extensive  study,  to 
testify  to  the  fidelity  and  value  of  a  "  History  of  Romanism." 

The  publisher  would  retuin  his  thanks  to  the  writers  of  manj^  kind  and  well- 
written  notices  which  have  been  necessarily  omitted  for  want  of  space.  To  have 
published  the  whole  without  abridgement  would  have  required  a  volume  ;  and  it 
was  thought  that  a  few  from  each  of  the  ranks  in  which  the  great  family  of  Protest- 
ant Christians  are  ranged,  would  be  more  easily  perused  than  ten  times  the  number, 
and  equally  efficacious.

ATTRACTIVE  NEW  WORK  ON   MISSIONS. 


E.  WALKER,  114  FULTON  STREET.  N.Y., 

HAS    RECENTLY   PUBLISHED.  IN   ONE    BEAUTIFUL    DUODECIMO  VOLUME,  ELEGANTLY 
PRINTED    ON    THE    FINEST    PAPER,    AND    SPLENDIDLY    BOUND, 
EXTRA    GILT,    EMBELLISHED    BY    A 

Superb  Illustration  executed  in  Oil  Colors, 

THE    MISSIONARY    MEMORIAL; 

Ql    £iterarn    rmi>  Eeiujiotts    Qonveniv. 

This  Volume  has  been  expressly  prepared  to  meet  the  demand  for  a  Religious 
Offering,  whose  contents  should  be  the  aim  of  a  higher  Standard  of  Literature,  and 
of  more  intrinsic  and  enduring  value  than  has  been  found  to  characterize  the  pro- 
ductions usually  devoted  to  this  purpose.  The  leading  idea  of  the  Work,  and 
which  confers  its  Title,  occupies  a  prominent,  portion  of  its  pages,  and  has  engaged 
some  of  the  most  eminent  theological  and  scholastic  pens  of  the  country ;  while 
many  rich  literary  pearls  will  be  found  decking  the  volume,  with  the  true  scintilla- 
tions of  genius.  It  is  therefore  earnestly  hoped  that  it  will  prove  acceptable  to  the 
great  body  of  the  Christian  community.

TESTIMONIALS 

FROM   ALL  PROTESTANT  DENOMINATIONS, 


IN    APPROBATION    OF 


BOWLING'S  HISTORY  OF  ROMANISM. 


OPINIONS  OF  EPISCOPALIANS. 

From  the  Protestant    Churchman  (New  York.) 

This  is  a  beautiful  volume  of  672  pages.  Good  service  has  been  done  for  the  cause  of 
truth  by  its  publication,  and  it  certainly  ought  to  secure  for  its  author  an  enviable  reputa- 
tion among  contributors  to  the  standard  literature  of  the  day.  We  think  that  it  cannot  but 
reflect  much  credit  on  the  skill,  patience,  industry  and  judgment  of  the  author.  In  such  a 
work,  we  look  for  a  history — veritable,  authentic  history,  and  not  the  fabrications  of  a  one- 
sided controversialist — not  the  conjectural  inventions  of  a  determined  and  bigoted  parti- 
zan — not  a  series  of  declamatory  tirades  against  what  the  Romish  Church  is  supposedto  be 
by  those  who  discard  her  doctrines  and  authority — but  a  plain,  unvarnished  history  of  what 
she  is  actually  by  her  own  admissions  and  practices — a  faithful  and  impartial  exhibition 
from  her  oion  archives,  of  her  recorded  and  attested  opinions  and  usages. 

We  go  to  their  records,  and  councils,  and  bulls,  and  proclamations,  and,  in  their  men 
language,  we  let  them  speak  for  themselves.  In  this  respect  it  seems  to  us  that  the  authoi 
has,  in  a  great  measure,  redeemed  the  pledge  given  in  the  second  page  of  his  preface. 
That  he  has  sometimes  permitted  Protestants  to  tell  their  stories,  too,  is  no  more  than  fair, 
so  long  as  they  are  well  authenticated  and  vouched  by  competent  witnesses. 

As  a  book  of  reference,  the  work  will  be  found  valuable.  The  evidence  of  what  Roman- 
ism is,  and  has  been, — evidence  which  until  now,  has  been  scattered  over  a  wide  field,  and 
embraced  in  an  immense  number  of  works  in  different  languages,  is  here  brought  together  and 
condensed  in  a  comparatively  small  compass  ;  and  those  who  wish  to  become  better  inform- 
ed on  Lhe  subject,  can  find  all  they  desire,  without  the  toil  and  expense  of  wading  through 
libraries  of  books,  and  composing  and  arraying  for  use,  the  scattered  materials  of  a  host  of 
writers.  The  author  appears  very  justly  to  have  conceived  the  idea  of  a  work,  which  has 
long  been  regarded  as  a  desideratum  by  a  large  portion  of  those  interested  in  the  great 
controversy  with  the  Romanists. 

The  plates  are  of  a  very  high  order,  the  execution  of  the  work,  reflects  gre  at  credit  on 
the  publisher,  and  its  general  merits  are  such,  that  we  trust  it  may  have  a  widecirculation. 

From  the  Episcopal  Recorder  (Philadelphia.) 

This  work  is  by  the  Rev.  John  Dowling,  A.M.,  Pastor  of  the  Bcrean  Church,  New 
\  oik.  It  traces  its  subject  from  the  earliest  corruptions  of  Christianity  to  the  present  time. 
It  lias  a  full  Chronological  Table,  with  Analytical  and  Alphabetical  Indexes  and  Glossary. 
It  is  ornamented  with  a  large  number  of  highly  finished  and  beautiful  engravings.  It  is  a 
large  and  beautiful  volume,  and  full  of  valuable  information  . 

OPINIONS  OF  LUTHERANS. 
K?  READ  THE  FOLLOWING. 

From  the  Rev.  L.  Giustiniani,  D.  D.,  formerly  a  Roman  Catholic  Priest  in  the  City  of 
Rome,  and  author  of  a  popular  work,  entitled  "Papal  Rome  as  it  is,"  now  a  Clergyman  oj 
the  Lutheran  Church  (published  in  the  Christian  Repository.) 

The  writer  of  the  following  interesting  letter,  was  born  and  educated  as  *.  Roman 
Catholic  Priest  at  the  city  of  Rome.  After  his  conversion,  which  wis  brought 
about  by  the  blessing  of  God,  upon  a  French  copy  of  "  Father  Clement,"  and  a 
copy  of  the  Bible,  which  providentially  fell  into  his  way  ;  he  fled  fiom  the  Papal 
dominions  to  Florence,  to  escape  the  vengeance  of  the  Roman  Inquisition,  to  which 
he  was  destined   by  his   popish  enemies.     At   Florence,  he  was  demanded   as   a 


LITERARY    NOTICES    OF 

subject  of  the  Pope,  but  again  escaped  to  Switzerland,  where  he  supported  himself 
for  some  lime,  by  lecturing  on  the  Oriental  languages,  in  the  college  of  Lausanne. 
He  subsequently  came  to  America,  and  was  admitted  as  a  member  of  the  Baltimore 
Svnodj  <»f  tin-  Lutheran  Protestant  church,  where  he  still  remains.  The  testimony 
of  Bach  ;i  man  relative  to  the  value  of  this  "History  of  Romanism"  is  of  great  worth. 

Mr.  Editor. — If  the  nineteenth  century  is  really  the  age  of  progress,  as  it  is  agreed 
upon,  the  doctrine  that  Popery  will  ultimately  sway  its  iron  sceptre,  and  fetter  the  con- 
sciences of  the  whole  human  race,  as  in  former  times,  must  be  rejected  as  absurd,  and 
rv  to  the  progressive  spirit  of  the  present  age. 

1  am  not  a  theorist  who  sets  down  systems  at  the  table  of  my  study  ;  I  am  not  a  politi- 
cian whs  frames  plans  for  his  party,  right  or  wrong,  just  or  unjust ;  I  am  speaking  of  my 
own  experience,  though,  vet  a  young  Protestant,  I  can  say  without  presumption  or  pride, 
that  1  know  sufficiently  of  the  power  of  Protestantism,  to  give  my  humble  opinion,  that 
Protestantism  will  and  must  sway  the  sceptre  of  truth  and  liberty  over  the  whole  world; 
and  if  Protestant  ministers  would  fearlessly  speak  what  they  know  of  the  man  of  sin,  and 
faithfully  set  before  the  eyes  of  the  American  people  the  immoral,  and  all-grasping  tendency 
of  Popery  in  the  United  States,  they  would  crush  the  papal  power  to  atoms,  exile  the 
foreign  influence  from  our  schools,  from  our  firesides  and  from  the  American  soil;  and  the 
sophistry  of  the  hundreds  of  Brownsons,  and  thousands  of  Hughes  would  perish  by  the 
annvs  nf  (ruth. 

A  tew  vears  ago,  when  I  left  the  church  of  Rome,  I  scarcely  found  a  dozen  of  Protest- 
ants in  a  city,  who  would  believe  me  when  I  recounted  the  corruptions  of  the  Roman 
clergy,  the  immoralities  of  nunneries  and  the  political  influence  of  Rome  in  Protestant 
countries.  The  pulpits  were  silent,  the  people  indifferent,  and  even  ignorant  on  that  topic. 
But  now  Protestant  ministers  are  writing  histories  of  Popery,  and  we  have  a  History  of 
li  imanism  before  us,  from  its  birth  to  its  grave,  from  its  cradle  to  its  manhood  ;  which 
every  Protestant  family  ought  to  possess  :  that  the  sons  and  daughters  of  American  parents 
!n;iv  read,  and  study  the  past,  and  take  necessary  measures  for  the  future. 

If  the  reader  wishes  to  be  acquainted  with  the  errors  of  Romanism,  he  has  only  to  open 
the  pagps  of  Dowlin^'s  History  ;  if  he  is  desirous  to  know  of  her  cruelties,  he  can  find  all 
in  that  work  ;  if  he  likes  to  know  of  her  soul-destroying  doctrines,  he  will  find  it  in  the 
decretals  of  the  principal  councils,  the  Lateran,  and  the  Tridentinum  included.  If  the 
r  is  anxious  to  read  an  epitome  of  the  history  of  the  popes,  and  their  corrupt  lives  ; 
of  their  inhuman  persecution  of  the  Waldenses  ;  their  ambition;  their  intrigues;  their 
avarieiousness  ;  their  tyranny  ;  their  blood-thirstiness  ;  their  superstitions,  and  their  mum- 
is,  he  can  find  all  in  Dowling's  History  proved  and  authenticated  by  the  most  accredited 
authors  of  the  Church  of  Rome. 

It  is  jrot  up  in  the  finest  style,  and  would  be  an  ornament  upon  every  centre  table  ;  useful 

in  every  family,  and  a  valuable  reference  book  in  every  library.     In  one  word  it  is  a  library 

HOT  a  book.     The  plates  are  well  executed;  I  have  seen  all  the  buildings,  sceneries, 

&  •  ,  and  was  an  eye  witness  of  all  these  ecclesiastical  functions,  or  rather  theatrical  per- 

:      nances,  and  am  delighted  to  see  them  so  faithfully  represented  in  the  plates. 

The  Hev.  Mr.  Bowling  will  pardon  me  for  the  liberty  I  have  taken  in  speaking  of  his 
work,  without  having  a  personal  acquaintance  with  him.  His  zeal  for  Protestantism  his 
love  to  America,  and  over  all  his  desire  to  promote  the  kingdom  of  our  Redeemer,  Jesus 
Christ,  constrain  me  to  bring  him  the  tribute  of  a  Protestant,  an  American  citizen,  and 
minister  iff  Christ  ;  and  if  my  critique  is  not  adequate  to  the  magnitude  and  extent  of  the 
work,  he  must  attribute  it  not  to  my  will,  but  to  my  insufficiency,  and  to  my  ignorance  of 
the  English  idiom. 

L.   GlUSTINIANI. 

Philadelphia,  August  4th,  1815. 

From  the  Lutheran  Observer. 

This  is  a  large  and  beautiful  volume,  containing  a  comprehensive  and  complete  history  of 

Romanism,  through  the  whole  period  of  its  existence.     In  many  other  works  the  errors  of 

the  papacy  are  discussed  and  refuted  ;  in  this,  the  author  relates  the  story  of  their  origin, 

progress  and   fruits,  as  developed   in  the   history  of  the  Romish  communion.     Here   the 

reader  may  see  its  unscriptnral  doctrines  and  ceremonies,  the  lives  of  its  most  distinguished 

papes,  the  proceedings  of  its  celebrated  councils,  the  details  of  its  tyranny  over  monarchs 

i;  its  inquisitions,  tortures,  and  massacres,  the  efforts  of  good  men  in  various  ages 

:ver  the  world  from  its  thraldom;  all  presented  in   their  chronological  order.     From  a 

partial  examination,  W8  think  it  it  an  able  work,  comprising  the  results  of  extensive  reading 

and  research,  and  well  adapted  to  fill  an  important  chasm  in  our  literature. 

It  is  printed  in  superior  style,  on  fair  type  and  paper,  and  embellished  with  more  than  fifty 


DOWLING  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

highly  finished  engravings,  which  illustrate  the  ceremonies,  superstitions,  persecutions,  and 
historical  incidents  of  Popery.  The  illustrations  which  »re  executed  in  the  most  elegant 
style,  are  vivid  representations  of  the  scenes  which  they  are  designed  to  portray.  The 
work  no  doubt  will  receive,  as  it  merits,  liberal  and  extensive  patronage. 

OPINIONS  OF  THE  DUTCH  REFORMED. 

From  the  New  York  Christian  Intelligencer. 

We  have  had  the  pleasure  of  examining  the  largest  part  of  Mr.  Bowling's  forthcoming 
work,  in  sheets,  and  regard  it  as  a  most  important  addition  to  the  historical  and  religious 
literature  of  the  age. 

.Mr.  Dowliug  is  already  favorably  known  to  the  reading  public  by  his  work  on  the 
Prophecies  of  Daniel  in  reply  to  Miller  ;  his  "  Defence  of  the  Protestant  Scriptures  in 
reply  to  Popish  apologists  for  the  Champlain  Bible-burners,"  &c.  In  the  present  extensive 
and  important  work,  he  has  performed  his  task  with  learning,  ability  and  tact,  and  has  suc- 
ceeded in  presenting  within  the  compass  of  a  single  volume,  a  clear,  succinct,  well  written 
and  well  authenticated  History  of  Romanism,  its  Ceremonies,  Doctrines,  Councils,  Popes, 
Inquisitions,  &c,  from  the  earliest  ages  down  to  the  present  time.  Heretofore,  in  order  to 
possess  an  adequate  knowledge  of  Popery  in  all  its  ages,  it  has  been  necessary  to  possess 
;  dd  to  study  a  library  of  books  on  the  subject.  In  this  work,  facts  scattered  through  hun- 
dreds of  volumes,  Roman  Catholic  as  well  as  Protestant,  and  many  of  them  in  the  Latin 
language,  have  been  brought  together  chronologically  arranged,  and  condensed,  and  related  in 
the  author's  pleasing  and  attractive  style  ;  thus  supplying  a  chasm  that  has  long  been 
felt  by  those  who  wished  to  become  familiar  with  that  subject,  which  is  destined  for  years 
to  come  to  be  the  great  subject  of  controversy  in  America — Romanism,  and  with  it  mental 
and  political  slavery  ;  or  Protestantism,  the  Bible,  and  with  it  liberty  of  thought,  of  worship, 
and  of  the  press. 

The  engravings  are  all  illustrative  of  some  facts  in  the  history  of  Romanism,  the  descrip- 
tion of  which  is  embodied  in  the  page  adjoining  the  engraving.  In  this  respect,  the  work 
differs  in  toto  from  the  mere  pictorial  works  of  the  day.  A  collection  of  pictorial  orna- 
ments, not  adapted  to  illustrate  important  facts,  and  of  which  no  description  is  given,  is 
litter  for  children  than  men.  In  the  present  work  the  engravings  have  been  prepared,  not 
as  mere  ornaments,  but  to  illustrate  the  facts,  and  thus  to  impress  them  more  strongly  on 
the  memory.  They  are  printed  by  an  artist  in  the  English  style  upon  plate  paper,  not  in 
the  way  most  common  in  America,  with  letter  press  on  the  back  of  the  same  leaf.  In  ar- 
tistical  and  mechanical  execution,  Mr.  Walker  has  spared  neither  labor  nor  expense  to 
make  this  volume  one  of  the  most  elegant  works  ever  issued  from  the  American   press. 

The  History  is  divided  into  nine  books,  each  book  subdivided  into  chapters  and  sections 
with  running  titles,  analytical  and  alphabetical  indices,  and  full  chronological  table  of  the 
Popes,  general  councils,  and  most  remarkable  events.  Each  Book  embraces  a  period,  and 
the  arrangement  is  original,  novel,  and  striking,  We  give  the  titles  of  two  or  three  of  the 
books  as  specimens.  The  title  of  the  first  book  is  "  Popery  in  Embryo,  from  the  first 
(iawn  of  papal  corruptions  till  the  establishment  of  the  papal  supremacy.  A.  D.  60(i.'' 
Of  the  fourth,  "  Popery  in  its  Glory;  the  World's  Midnight,  from  the  Coronation 
of  Charlemagne,  A.  D.  800,  to  the  pontificate  of  Pope  Hildebrand  or  Gregory  VII.,  A.D., 
!  073."  Of  the  fifth,  'Popery,  the  World's  Despot,  from  the  accession  of  Pope 
Gregory  VII.,  A.  D.  1072,  to  the  death  of  Boniface  VHP,  1303."  In  the  seventh  book, 
entitled  Popery  at  Trent,  he  has  given  the  citation  of  the  most  important  of  the  decrees 
and  the  canons  and  curses  of  the  Council  of  Trent,  in  parallel  Columns  of  Latin  and  Eng- 
iii  arrangement  adopted,  as  Mr.  Dowling  informs  us,  in  order  to  obviate  the  common 
objection  of  Romanists  of  inaccurate  translations.  The  same  plan  is  adopted  in  citing 
from  several  other  original  authorities.  This  will  much  enhance  the  value  of  the  work  in 
the  eyes  of  ministers  and  theological  students,  while  it  will  not  diminish  the  value  to  any, 
as  the  English  translation  is  invariably  given. 

From  a  subsequent  notice  in  the  same  paper. 
Having  previously  apprised  our  readers  of  the  preparation  of  this  invaluable  work,  we 
need  do  little  more  than  announce  the  fact  that  it  is  now  ready  for  delivery.  It  is  an  octavo 
\.<iume  of  670  pages,  with  admirable  typography,  on  beautiful  paper,  and  in  superbly  gilt 
binding.  Its  contents  form  a  rich  storehouse  of  historical  instruction,  which,  if  it  could  be 
placed  within  the  reach  of  every  family,  would  prove  an  unspeakable  blessing  to  our 
beloved  country.  This  is  the  book  for  Americans.  Americans  must  have  books,  and  of 
the  right  kind.  Americans  must  read.  The  man  who  neglects  it,  does  it  at  his  own  peril, 
and  at  the  peril  of  his  country.  Every  American  patriot  should  thoroughly  study  the  long 
established  and  unchangeable  principles  and  practices  of  Romanism.     It  should  be  known 


LITERARY    NOTICES    OF 

what  this  Mystery  of  Iniquity  has  ever  done  in  ages  past — what  it  is  now  doing  wherever 
civil  restraint  does  not  intimidate  and  hinder  ;  and  it  should  be  distinctly  understood  that  it 
is  immutable.  It  lays  claim  to  infallibility,  and  ever  spurns  the  idea  of  the  possibility  of 
change.  Those  who  would  see  a  correct  and  condensed  exhibition  of  these,  and  the  ten 
thousand  other  abominations  of  the  Papacy,  may  find  it,  to  their  amazement,  in  "Bowling's 
History  of  Romanism."  The  author,  as  it  seems,  has  availed  himself  of  all  the  standard 
and  authentic  works  on  General  and  Ecclesiastical  History,  on  the  Inquisition  and  perse- 
cutions of  Popery,  on  the  Reformers  and  the  Reformation,  and  on  the  points  of  controversy 
between  Popery  and  Protestantism. 

Had  we  room  to  spare,  we  would  gladly  make  some  extracts  ;  but  as  it  is,  we  must  refer 
our  readers  to  the  work  itself.  For  Auricular  confession,  see  pages  517  and  518.  On  the 
Inquisition,  pages  570-573.  For  the  Slaughter  of  the  Waldenses,  see  page  582.  In  a 
word,  see  the  entire  volume.  Place  it  beside  your  Bibles.  Compare  its  records  of  crime 
and  the  Papal  principles  it  exposes,  with  the  holy  doctrines  and  immaculate  life  of  Jesus  of 
Nazareth.  When  you  have  done  this,  you  will  have  no  difficulty  in  determining  whether 
or  not  Romanism  is  Christianity. 

From  the  Rev.  W.  C.  Broionlee  D.D.,  New  York. 

I  have  examined  the  work  entitled,  "  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism,  Illustrated." 
I  consider  it  one  of  the  most  valuable  works  on  the  subject,  that  I  have  ever  perused  :  and 
trust  it  will  meet  with  an  extensive  circulation.  W.  C.  BROWNLEE. 

Fmm  the  Rev.  Joseph  Wilson,  D.D.,  Pastor  of  the  Reformed  Dutch  Church,  Tarrytown. 

I  have  carefully  read  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism,  and  can  cheerfully  recom- 
mend it  to  my  fellow  Protestants,  as  an  intsructive  and  faithful  exhibition  of  Popery,  from 
its  origin  down  to  the  present  time.  It  is  a  book  that  should  be  in  the  hands  of  every 
one  desirous  of  obtaining  a  perfect  knowledge  of  the  great  Apostasy.         J.  WILSON. 

OPINIONS  OF  THE  PRESBYTERIANS  AND  CONGREGA- 

TIONALISTS. 

From  the  New  York  Evangelist,  (Rev.  Dr.  George  B.  Cheeier,  Editor.) 
We  ought  to  have  noticed  this  excellent  and  beautiful  work  before.  It  possesses  many 
and  very  strong  claims  for  popular  favor,  and  we  do  not  doubt  that  it  is  destined  to  have 
an  extraordinary  sale.  It  is  one  of  the  most  elegantly  embellished  and  executed  works  we 
have  ever  seen  issued  from  the  American  press;  containing  a  large  number  of  highly 
finished  engravings,  illustrative  of  the  ceremonies,  superstitions,  persecutions  and  incidents 
of  Romanism,  which  often  tell  a  most  eloquent  tale,  and  are  of  real  utility  as  well  as  highly 
ornamental.  The  work  itself  is  characterized  by  great  research,  and  a  comprehensive  and 
Scriptural  view  of  the  nature  and  history  of  the  Popish  system.  It  presents  a  succinct, 
but  sufficiently  full,  history  of  the  rise,  progress,  errors,  ciuelties,  and  present  condition  of 
the  Papacy,  authenticated  by  reference  to  the  most  undoubted  historic  sources,  related  in  a 
spirited,  engaging  and  impressive  style,  and  arranged  in  the  most  lucid  manner.  It 
abounds  in  facts  and  incidents,  and,  with  its  beautiful  illustrations,  is  better  adapted  to  fur- 
nish a  vivid  and  impressive  portraiture  of  Rotnanism  as  it  is,  than  any  other  book  we 
know  of.  It  supplies  a  great  lack  in  our  theological  literature  ;  and  now  that  a  conflict 
with  Rome  is  forced  upon  us,  and  is  soon  to  become  the  business  of  us  all,  clergy  and  laity, 
a  work  so  richly  stored  with   facts  and  arguments  will  be  most   timely  and   useful.      We 

SHOULD  BE  01.  YD  TO  SEE  IT  IN  EVERY  FAMILY,  AND  ADORNING  EVERY  CENTRE-TABLE  IN 
THE    LAND. 

From  the  Philadelphia  Presbyterian. 

This  work  exhibits  a  comprehensive  view  of  Romanism — its  doctrines  and  ceremonies, 
its  politico-religious  machinery,  its  popes  and  councils,  its  intolerant  spirit,  and  its  murder- 
ous practices — from  its  first  origin  to  the  present  day.  Such  a  work  required  deep  and 
protracted  research,  and  it  affords  evidence  that  diligence,  caution,  and  judgment,  in  the 
selection  and  arrangement  of  materials,  have  not  been  wanting.  The  author  has  availed 
himself  of  the  writings  of  both  Protestants  and  Papists,  and  has  brought  together  a  greater 
mass  of  information  on  the  history,  the  spirit,  and  the  doings  of  Popery,  than  we  have  ever 
seen  before  in  one  volu  ne.  When  the  enemy  is  coming  in  upon  us  like  a  flood,  it  is  time  to 
lift  up  such  a  standard.  The  deluded  votaries  of  the  Man  of  Sin  may  raise  the  cry  of  per- 
secution, and  recreant  Protestants  may  join  in  the  cry,  but  all  who  value  the  truth  of  God, 
and  the  civil  and  religious  independence  of  our  country,  will  hail  the  appearance  of  such  a 
woik. 


DOWLING'S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

From  the  New  York  Observer. 
Dowling's  History  of  Romanism. — In  one  an  some  octavo  volume  we  have  here  a 
complete  history  of  Romanism  from  the  earliest  corruptions  of  Christianity  to  the  present 
time.  It  is  prepared  by  the  Rev.  J.  Dowling  of  this  city,  and  published  by  E.  Walker,  and 
we  presume  will  be  circulated  very  widely,  as  it  gives,  in  popular  style  and  comprehensive 
form,  such  a  history  of  Popery  as  cannot  be  found  by  the  general  reader.  We  think  the 
book  cannot  fail  to  attract  general  attention.  The  engravings  with  which  it  is  profusely 
illustrated  are  by  Lossing,  and  are  beautiful  specimens  of  the  art. 

From  the  Rev.  Edwin  F.   Hatfield,  Pastor  of  the  Broome  Street  Presbyterian  Church, 

New  York 

Among  the  candidates  for  public  patronage  which  a  press  of  extraordinary  prolificacy  is 
throwing  out  upon  the  world,  we  have  of  late  seen  nothing  to  excel  this  production  of  Mr. 
Dowling's.  Its  typography  is  of  remarkable  accuracy  and  beautiful  finish.  Its  illustrations, 
fifty-two  in  number,  are  also  of  a  high  order  and  truly  appropriate.  No  pains  appear  to 
have  been  spared  in  the  mechanical  execution  of  the  book,  to  make  it  attractive  to  all 
classes. 

But,  after  all,  these  are  matters  of  inferior  importance.  Many  a  book  of  beautiful 
exterior  is  on  the  whole  perfectly  worthless.  We  are  happy  to  commend  the  author  in 
the  present  case,  as  well  as  the  publisher.  On  a  subject  so  trite  as  Romanism,  it  requires 
no  small  tact,  talent,  and  industry,  to  present  anything  of  peculiar  worth  to  a  reading  com- 
munity ;  and  yet  Mr.  Dowling  has  done  it.  A  glance  at  his  quotations,  authorities,  and 
table  of  contents,  shows  that  he  has  diligently  investigated  his  subject,  and  studied  well  I  ■ 
ever-varying  aspects  of  an  unvarying  superstition.  He  has  siezed  upon  an  admirable  concep- 
tion in  presenting  these  aspects, — "  Popery  in  Embryo; — at  its  Birth  ; — Advancing  ; — in  its 
Glory  ; — the  World's  Despot ; — on  a  Tottering  Throne  ; — at  Trent ; — Drunk  with  the  Blood 
of  the  Saints; — and  in  its  Dotage."  In  exhibiting  these  several  stages  of  Popery,  he  has 
brought  together  an  array  of  facts  well-substantiated,  of  deep  interest,  and  of  vast  importance. 
The  facts  we  have  examined  somewhat  minutely  in  parts  of  the  work,  and  have  satisfied 
ourselves  that  Mr.  D.,  is  at  home  in  researches  of  this  nature.  We  feel  under  many  obli- 
gations to  him  for  furnishing  us  with  such  a  book  of  references — a  book  with  such  a  minute 
analysis  preceding  it;  such  a  particular  caption  for  every  page,  and  such  a  full  index  at  the 
close ;  matters  very  much  neglected  in  American  books,  but  never  lost  sight  of  by  German 
Scholars. 

Nearly  twenty  years  ago,  "The  Christian  Spectator"  of  New  Haven,  ventured  the 
remark  (VIII.,  410),  "  In  the  United  States  we  have  nothing  to  fear  from  Catholic  influ- 
ence." Sad  experience  has  shown  the  contrary.  On  every  side  are  rising  Papal  Nun- 
neries, Academies,  Colleges,  Theological  Seminaries,  and  houses  of  worship.  Every 
packet-ship  almost  is  bringing  over  a  fresh  importation  of  papal  emissaries,  male  and 
female  ;  and  every  passenger-ship  its  scores  and  hundreds  of  papal  immigrants.  So  that 
we  can  now  say — "  In  the  United  States  we  have  everything  to  fear  from  Catholic  influ- 
ence." We  welcome  such  a  book  as  Mr.  Dowling's  with  all  our  heart,  and,  if  we  could, 
would  introduce  it  into  every  family  in  the  land. 

EDWIN  F.  HATFIELD. 

From  the  Rev,  E.   W.  Andrews,  of  the  Presbyterian  Church,  Troy,  late  Pastor  oj  the 

Broadway  Tabernacle,  New  York. 
As  far  as  I  have  been  able  to  examine,  I  am  highly  pleased  with  it,  both  in  its  design 
and  execution.  As  a  History,  it  seems  to  be  impartial  and  accurate  in  its  statements,  and 
is  sufficiently  copious  in  its  details  for  all  the  ends  of  a  popular  work.  It  is  timely  in  its 
appearance,  and  will  fill,  to  the  satisfaction  of  all  true  Protestants,  a  vacancy  which  has 
long  been  felt  to  exist  in  the  historical  literature  of  our  language.  The  manner  in  which 
the  work  has  been  presented  to  the  public,  reflects  great  credit  on  the  taste  and  skill  of  the 
enterprising  publisher. 

E.  W.  ANDREWS. 
From  the  Rev.  S.  G.  Speer,  Troy. 
The  subject  of  which  Mr,  Dowling  treats  is  one  of  immense  and  immediate  interest.  It 
ought  to  be  well  understood  by  every  man  in  America.  It  relates  to  matters  upon  which 
Papists  and  Protestants  must  soon  join  issue,  not  theoretically  alone,  but  practically,  and  in 
earnest.  Read,  citizens  of  this  threatened  republic,  and  understand  for  yourselves.  Mr. 
Dowling  has  done  a  great  work,  he  writes  chronologically,  logically,  and  with  the  force  of 
resistless  truth.  It  is  already  adjudged  worthy  of  the  merit  of  a  standard  book.  Read  it 
every  man  that  can  buy  or  borrow  it.  S.  G.   SPEER, 

Pastor  of  the  fourth  Church,  Troy,  New  York.

From  the  Boston  Recorder. 


The  first  thing  that  catches  the  eye  on  taking  up  this  book,  is  its  beautiful  exterior,  ele- 
pance  and  strength  combined.  On  opening  it,  the  engravings  arrest  attention  ;  of  these 
are  fifty  two  on  steel,*  illustrating  the  ceremonies,  superstitions,  persecutions,  histori- 
ral  incidents,  &c,  of  Romanism.  In  the  mode  of  bringing  together  and  presenting  the 
facts  of  popish  history,  it  will  be  conceded  we  think,  that  Mr.  Dowling  has  excelled.  He 
begins  with  the  embryo  state  of  this  great  error,  and  traces  it  down  in  chronological 
order,  ending  with  its  latest  manifestations  in  this  country  and  in  Europe.  Such  a  work 
was  wanted.  The  detached  portions  of  Romish  history  which  were  everywhere  to  be 
met  with,  needed  to  be  brought  together  and  presented  in  systematic  order.  The  reading 
of  one  book  thus  becomes  better  and  cheaper  than  the  reading  of  many.  The  alphabetical 
indexes,  glossary,  and  chronological  table,  will  be  found  a  valuable  addition  to  the  work. 

*  They  are  on  wood  by  Lossing,  but  others  beside  the  Recorder  have  made  the  same  mistake. 

From  the  Rev.  W.  L.  McCalla,  D.D.,  Philadelphia. 

A  handsome  volume,  fit  to  adorn  the  library  of  any  gentleman  or  lady  ;  and  within  the 
reach  of  many  a  poor  man,  thirsty  of  knowledge  ;  large,  but  convenient ;  easily  opened, 
and  lying  open  where  you  please.  Look  into  it,  and  its  open  countenance  is  like  that  of  an 
intelligent  traveller,  with  whom  you  become  acquainted  directly.  Candid,  affable  and  kind, 
he  gives  a  direct  and  satisfactory  answer  to  any  question.  It  is  a  kind  Providence  which 
sends  such  a  work  at  this  time.  W.  L.  McCALLA 

Philadelphia  July  30th,  1845.

OPINIONS  OF  METHODISTS. 

From  the  Christian  Advocate  and  Journal  {New  York.) 

An  elegant  volume  of  672  pages,  octavo,  printed  on  good  paper,  with  large,  clear,  reada- 
ble type,  and  illustrated  with  about  fifty  spirited  engravings,  illustrative  of  some  prominent 
facts  in  the  History  of  Romanism,  the  description  of  which  is  embodied  in  the  page  adjoin- 
ing the  engraving.  The  History  is  divided  into  nine  books,  the  titles  of  which  will  not  be 
easily  forgotten,  when  the  facts  are  laid  up  in  the  memory — "  Popery  in  embryo," — "  Pope- 
ry in  its  glory,  or  the  World's  Midnight,"  "  Popery,  the  World's  Despot,"  &c.  The  ex- 
pense of  this  work  will  be  great,  and  we  really  hope  that  Mr.  Walker  will  meet  with  an 
adequate  compensation  for  his  well  meant  and  timely  effort  to  expose,  not  "one  of  the 
greatest,"  but  the  greatest  abomination  that  was  ever  palmed  upon  the  world. 

From  Z  ion's  Herald  (Boston.) 

A  fine  large  volume,  giving1,  in  considerable  detail,  the  history  of  the  Papacy  from  the  be- 
ginning down  to  the  present  time,  including  the  late  movements  in-England  and  Germany, 
with  full  chronological  tallies  and  analytical  indices.  It  supplies  a  desideratum.  We  have 
full  discussions  of  the  doctrines  and  usages  of  I'operv,  but  no  comprehensive  and,  at  the  same 
time,  popular  hiitorf  of  it.  Mr.  Dowling's  work  includes  the  leading  events  of  its  his- 
tory— its  corruptions,  especially,  are  exposed  with  an  unsparing  hand,  and  form  a  picture 
of  appalling  enormity.  It  is  written  in  a  popular  style.  Its  engravings  .are  numerous,  and 
finely  executed.  The  book  will  doubtless  be  popular,  and  cannot  fail  to  produce  a  pro- 
found conviction  of  the  terrible  iniquity  of  anti-Christ.

Assistant  Editor  of  the  Christian  Advocate. 

OPINIONS  OF  BAPTISTS. 

From  the  Rev.  Spencer  H.  Cone,  D.D.,  Neiv  York. 

"The  History  of  Romanism"  by  my  excellent  friend  and  brother, the  Rev.  John  Dow- 
ling  of  NewYork,  is  quite  equal  to  my  expectations,  when  occasionally  examining  a  proof 
sheet,  or  an  engraving,  as  the  work  was  passing  through  the  press.  Since  its  publication,  I 
have  looked  over  the  volume,  and  am  much  pleased  with  its  embellishments,  and  typograph- 
ical execution.  Its  matter  is  deeply  interesting  and  highly  important,  embracing  an  array 
of  historical  facts  which  exhibit  "the  man  of  sin"'  in  his  true  character,  a  character  directly 
opposed  to  the  happiness  of  the  human  family,  and  to  the  mind  and  example  of  our  adorable 
Saviour,  who  instead  of  destroying  men's  lives,  "  went  about  doing  good."  The  glossary, 
tables,  and  indexes,  will  enable  the  reader  to  find  without  diltlculty  or  delay,  any  fact  the 
details  of  which  he  may  at  the  moment  wish  to  know  :  and  the  practical  use  of  the  work  is 
hereby  greatly  enhanced.  We  cordially  commend  it  to  our  fellow-citizens,  not  only  for 
its  intrinsic  worth,  but  its  peculiar  adaptation  to  "  Our  Times,"  when  so  many  are  teaching 
for  doctrines  the  commandments  of  men.  SPENCER  II.  CONE, 

Pastor  of  the  first  Baptist  Church  N.  Y. 

From  the  Rev.  Rufus  Bahcocli,  D.D.,  late  President  of  Waierrille  College.) 

I  have  examined  with  interest  and  carefulness  a  large  part  of  the  volume  recently  pub- 
lished by  Mr.  Walker,  entitled  "  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism."  I  find  that  it? 
beautiful  and  expensive  illustrations,  are  among  its  least  recommendations.  The  author 
appears  to  have  brought  to  the  execution  of  this  great  work,  unwearied  industry,  genuine 
and  thorough  scholarship,  and  scrupulous  fidelity,  The  ascertainment  and  developement 
of  truth,  is  his  great  object,  as  is  every  where  apparent.  Hence  the  care  he  has  evinced 
to  adduce  the  original  text  of  his  authorities  in  very  many  instances,  side  by  side  with  the 
translation  of  thern  furnished  for  the  benefit  of  unlearned  readers.  In  no  one  instance,  so 
far  as  I  have  examined,  is  there  any  thing  in  these  translations  which  a  hypercritical  op- 
ponent can  object  to.  This  candor  and  fairness,  is  of  the  utmost  iinportanee  under  such 
circumstances,  and  cannot  fail  to  render  this  volume  a  standard  authority  in  the  great 
controversy  which  the  signs  of  the  times  indicate  as  now  near  at  hand.  If  every  intelli- 
gent Protestant  would  make  himself  master  of  the  facts,  here  luminously  presented,  and 
arranged,  he  would  be  ready  to  meet  the  wily  advocates  of  Romish  error  in  every  Protean 
form  it  may  assume.  RUFUS  BABCOCK. 

From  Rev.  Benjamin  M.  Hill,  Secretary  of  the  American  Baptist  Home  Mission  Society. 

I  have  examined  the  History  of  Romanism,  by  Rev.  Mr.  Dowling,  with  much  satisfaction. 
It  embodies  a  great  amount  and  variety  of  matter,  connected  with  the  rise  and  progress  of 
Romanism,  with  which  all  should  be  familiar,  and  which  may  be  profitably  used  on  many 
occasions.  The  arrangement  and  style  cannot  fail  to  assist  the  reader  in  acquiring  and 
remembering  important  facts. 

Among  its  excellencies  I  consider  the  numerous  quotations  from  Catholic  authorities  as 
one  of  much  importance,  as  they  will  often  prove  a  convenience  to  those  who  may  happen 
to  be  destitute  of  the  original  works  ;  and  that  importance  is  increased  by  the  English 
translations,  which  are  arranged  in  parallel  columns  with  them,  thus  affording  facility  for 
testing  the  faithfulness  of  the  translation. 

While  the  book  will  supply  a  deficiency  in  our  literature  upon  that  subject,  of  great  im- 
portance at  the  present  time,  in  every  part  of  our  country,  I  think  it  will  prove  especially 
useful  in  /.lie  valley  of  the  Mississippi,  where  Romanists  are  making  great  efforts  to  obtain 
power,  and  where  many  evangelical  ministers  and  others  suffer  inconvenience  on  account 
of  their  scanty  libraries.  BENJAMIN  M.   HILL. 

New  York,  August  15th,  1845. 


LITERARY    NOTICES    OF    DOWLINO  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

From  the  Rev.  William  Shadrach,  Pastor  of  Sansom  street  Church,  Philadelphia. 

Edward  Walker,  Esq.  —  Dear  Sir:  Having  been  favored  with  the  perusal  of  "Bow- 
line's History  of  Romanism,"  I  take  great  pleasure  in  adding  my  testimonial  to  that  of 
others  in  commendation  of  the  work.  The  author  has  succeeded  admirably,  I  think,  in  the 
accomplishment  of  the  end  tie  proposed,  viz.,  to  fiill  a  chasm  which  had  been  long  felt  to 
exist  in  this  department  of  the  historical  and  religious  literature  of  the  age. 

I  consider  the  history  as  truthful,  fair  and  impartial  ;  it  is  concise,  but  comprehensive  ; 
and  contains  a  vast  fund  of  information  upon  a  subject  of  great  moment  to  every  American 
citizen.  The  facts  are  amply  sustained  by  undoubted  authorities.  Much  skill  is  evinced 
in  the  arrangement  of  topics.  The  style  is  clear,  cogent  and  perspicuous,  while  the  artistic 
embellishments  and  mechanical  execution  of  the  entire  work  reflects  the  highest  credit  upon 
the  taste  and  enterprize  of  the  publisher. 

The  book  is  truly  a  book  for  the  times.  Let  us  know  what  Rome  is  ;  and  if  "  history  is 
philosophy  teaching  by  example,"  let  us  carefully  study  her  lessons,  that  we  may  be  wise 
tu  our  choice.  I  sincerely  hope  that  the  work  may  have  a  wide  circulation,  and  with  kin- 
dred productions,  lead  the  American  public  to  improve  the  day  of  our  merciful  visitation, 
and  to  stand  forth  in  the  defence  of  our  liberties  and  the  august  rights  of  Man. 

Very  respectfully  yours,  &c, 

Philadelphia,  Sept.  6,  1845.  WILLIAM  SHADRACH. 

From  the  New  York  Recorder. 
Whatever  relates  to  the  history  of  the  Papal  church,  cannot  fail  to  have  even  peculiar 
interest  in  the  times  on  which  we  are  fallen.  The  present  signs  of  the  religious  world 
indicate  the  approach  of  a  moral  contest  with  the  Papacy,  such  as  there  has  not  been  since 
the  days  of  the  Reformation.  Under  this  view,  the  appearance  of  the  work,  whose  title  is 
given  above,  is  opportune.  It  is  written  with  the  ready,  popular  eloquence,  for  which  the 
well-known  author  is  distinguished,  and  cannot  fail  to  arrest  attention  to  the  controversy  of 
which  it  treats.  It  is  a  strongly  Protestant  work,  and  exhibits  the  deformities  of  Popery 
with  great  power.  Even  those  who  are  reluctant  to  admit  that  Popery  could  ever  have 
wrought  itself  out  in  the  horrors  which  have  been  charged  upon  it,  will  be  constrained  to 
acknowledge  the  dangers  of  the  Papal  system,  and  to  dread  its  further  extension.  As  a 
weapon  for  popular  controversy,  Mr.  Dowling's  work  has  peculiar  adaptations,  and  will 
command  a  very  extensive  circulation.     The  work  is  elegantly  printed  and  embellished. 

From  the  Baptist  Record  {Philadelphia). 

This  is  a  splendid  book  and  splendidly  illustrated.  A  work  supplying  a  vacancy  in  the 
religious  literature  of  the  age,  which  has  long  been  deplored  by  intelligent  Protestants. 

Thank-,  to  Mr.  Dowling  for  this  happy  result  of  his  devoted  labours,  and  the  Protestant 
public  will  have  cause  to  thank  him  too  Let  it  be  circulated.  Let  it  be  read  the  land 
over,  until  a  know 'edge  of  its  instructive  facts,  its  authentic  incidents,  its  historical  truths, 
rmd  its  well  digested  and  elaborate  accounts  of  the  unscriptural  doctrines,  the  superstitions 
ceremonies,  the  abominable  practices  of  the  Church  of  Rome  are  so  indelibly  and  vividly 
■  i  ii  upon  the  mind  that  the  true  and  unchanging  character  of  Popery  will  be  clearly 
known  to  every  one,  and  its  hierarchal  despotism  receive  a  blow  that  will  ultimately  prove 
its  death. 

This  work  is  a  faithful  history  of  Popery.  The  author  has  given  the  subject  years  of  re- 
l  on  and  siuily,  and  has  written  with  energy,  precision  and  perspicuity.  We  cheerfully 
recommend  this  History  to  the  Protestant  community,  believingthat  it  is  designed  to  accom- 
plish great  good,  and  overcome  a  prejudice  which  we  fear  is  every  day  becoming  more 
and  more  prevalent,  viz.,  that  the  Church  of  Rome  in  the  present  day  is  not  that  Apostate 
blood-stained  and  blood-thirsty  thing  it  was  in  the  days  of  Queen  Mary.  But  Mr.  Dowling 
proves  clearly  and  distinctly  from  History  that  Popery  is  unchanged. 

From  the  Christian  Watchman  (Boston.) 

This  work  which  we  announced  some  time  ago,  has  appeared  in  a  very  handsomely 
printed  and  illustrated  royal  octavo  volume  of  near  700  pages.  Such  a  work  has  long  been 
needed,  and  we  are  glad  that  one  so  well  qualified  for  the  task  as  Mr.  Dowling  has  under- 
taken to  supply  it  Commencing  with  the  early  history  of  Christianity,  the  writer  traces 
out  the  steps  by  which  the  papal  assumptions  advanced  to  that  extent  of  usurpation  and 
corruption  which  have  marked  i\>-  progress  in  latter  ages.  In  doing  this  he  has  been  oblig- 
ed to  remove  many  scenes  of  vice  and  profligacy  which  exhibit  Popery  in  all  its  deformity 
and  loathsomeness. 

Much  credit  is  due  to  the  enterprising  publisher,  Mr.  E.  Walker,  for  the  elegant  style 
in  which  the  book  is  presented  to  the  public. 


DOWLING  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

From  the  Christian  Reflector  (Boston.) 
The  arrangement  is  lucid,  the  facts  are  well  attested,  the  style  is  vigorous,  and  the  sub- 
ject is  one  of  surpassing  interest.  We  have  read  several  pages,  and  turned  to  different 
parts  of  the  book,  and  have  found  nothing  dry — nothing  dull.  The  issue  of  such  a  book  is 
timely.  Of  its  usefulness  there  cannot  be  a  doubt.  Its  comprehensiveness,  embracing  as 
it  does  every  fact  of  moment,  gives  it  superior  advantages  over  any  other  history  for 
general  reading  or  ordinary  reference.  We  congratulate  our  brother,  the  esteemed  author, 
on  having  so  well  accomplished  so  important  a  work,  and  on  having  so  tasteful  and  enter- 
prising a  publisher  to  give  the  results  of  his  labor  to  the  world. 

From  the  Rev.  Leland  Howard,  Pastor  of  the  North  Baptist  Church,  Troy,  New  York. 

Dowling's  History  of  Romanism  is  precisely  what  is  wanted ;  every  lover  of  truth, 
liberty,  and  human  weal,  should  possess,  study,  and  circulate  it.  Popery,  as  a  whole  and  in  its 
several  parts  is  traced  ;  in  its  for  ma  lien,  fulness,  and  its  working,  in  a  most  accurate,  lucid, 
and  convincing  manner.  The  book  cannot  be  too  strongly  recommended,  to  the  studious 
attention  of  all.  LELAND   HOWARD. 

From  the  Rev.  Aaron  Perkins,  Pastor  of  the  Baptist   Church  Poughkeepsie,  late  of  New 

York  City. 

I  have  just  finished  a  reading  of  "Dowling's  History  of  Romanism."  A  work 
of  the  kind  was  much  needed  ;  and  I  am  happy  to  be  able  to  say  in  all  honesty, 
that  in  my  judgment,  it  is  well  suited  to  the  present  crisis,  and  deserves,  as  I  trust 
it  will  receive,  a  wide  circulation.  I  am  particularly  pleased  with  two  principal  sections 
of  the  book.  It  was  very  important,  in  the  present  stage  of  Romanism,  to  spread  out,  and 
place  within  the  reach  of  our  citizens  in  genera],  a  pretty  full  account,  of  the  council  of 
Trent  :  particularly  as  this  was  the  last  general  council  of  the  church  of  Rome,  and  conse- 
quently presents  a  fair  view  of  her  faith  and  practice  at  the  present  day  :  and  identifies  her, 
as  the  mystery,  Babylon  of  the  Apocalypse,  drunken  with  the  blood  of  saints  and  martyrs. 
The  other  section  to  which  I  refer,  is  that  which  presents  some  of  the  leading  features  of 
Romanism  in  England.       ************ 

The  esteemed  author,  and  enterprising  publisher  may  rest  assured,  that  so  far  as  my  in- 
fluence goes,  it  will  be  in  favor  of  the  wide  circulation,  of  this  invaluable  "  History  of 
Romanism." 

OPINIONS  OF  OTHER  STANDARD  PERIODICALS,  RELIGIOUS 

OR  LITERARY. 

From  the  Albany  Religious  Spectator. 
This  work  cannot  fail  to  meet  with  a  cordialwelcome  from  every  true  friend  of  Protestant- 
ism. It  goes  back  to  the  very  birth  of  Popery,  and  shows  us  the  process  of  its  growth,  the 
influences  by  which  it  has  been  sustained,  and  the  substantial  identity  of  its  character  from 
the  beginning  to  the  present  hour.  It  is  a  work  of  most  laborious  research,  and  relies  for 
its  statements  chiefly  upon  authorities  which  the  Romanists  themselves  will  not  question. 
It  is  full  of  engraved  illustrations,  which  are  executed  with  great  beauty,  and  by  which 
many  of  the  statements  are  rendered  far  more  vivid  and  impressive.  We  doubt  whether 
there  is  another  work  from  which  so  much  may  be  learned  of  the  history,  the  character, 
the  tendencies  of  Romanism,  and  withal  at  so  cheap  a  rate,  as  from  this.  While  we  cherish, 
and  would  encourage,  nothing  but  good  will  towards  the  Romanists,  we  cannot  but  rejoice, 
as  Protestants,  in  the  circulation  of  books,  which  like  the  present,  are  adapted  to  stay  the 
progress  of  their  errors,  and  bring  out  to  the  world,  even  the  most  revolting  secrets  of 
their  system. 

From  the  Protestant  Banner  (Philadelphia.) 
Dowling's  History  of  Romanism  is  a  large  8vo. ,  of  672,  pages,  illustrated  with  upwards  of 
fifty  engravings  in  the  best  style  of  art.  It  presents  a  compendious  history  of  Romanism 
from  the  earliest  corruption  of  Christianity  to  the  present  time,  with  full  chronological  table, 
analytical  and  alphabetical  indexes  and  glossary.  From  the  examination  which  we  have 
been  able  to  afford  its  pages,  it  seems  to  us  the  most  elaborate  popular  work  on  the  subject 
which  has  appeared  in  this  country.     We  bespeak  for  it  an  extensive  circulation. 

From  the  Christian  Register. 
This  is  an  octavo  volume  of  some  670  pages.     It  is  very  handsomely  got  up,  and  is 
adorned  and  rendered  additionally  valuable  by  numerous  well  executed  engravings.     It   is 
written  in  a  good  style,  and  the  impression  a  cursory  exploration  has  made  upon  us  is  quite 
favorable.     It  contains  a  good  amount  of  matter,  well  arranged. 


LITERARY    NOTICES    OF 

From  the  Boston  Trumpet. 

Mr.  Dowling  has  consulted  largely  the  authorities  both  of  the  Catholic  and  Protestant 
Church.  Popery  has  been  permitted  to  speak  for  itself  for  the  purpose  of  obviating  the 
common  objection  of  Romanists  of  inaccurate  translations.  The  engravings,  of  whichthere 
are  a  great  number,  over  fifty,  we  believe,  are  of  the  first  order,  and  have  been  executed  by 
Mr.  B.  J.  Lossing,  of  New  York,  and  are  not  mere  fancy  sketches,  but  are  founded  on 
unquestionable  facts.  They  give  the  work  a  very  rich  appearance  ;  and  the  historical  facts 
connected  with  each  plate  are  given  on  the  adjoining  page. 

From  Righler's  National  Protestant. 

Rev.  Mr.  Dowling,  of  this  city,  has  lately  prepared  a  new  and  complete  History  of 
Romanism,  from  the  earliest  time  to  the  present.  It  has  been  compiled  with  great  fairness 
and  care,  from  the  most  authentic  and  historic  sources;  and  every  statement  is  fortified  by 
an  appeal  to  authorities.  It  is  composed  in  a  lively  and  elegant  style,  and  with  an  earnest 
Protestant  spirit,  and  forms  a  most  attractive  and  affecting  work.  Its  value  is  also  greatly 
enhanced  by  a  large  number  of  beautiful  engravings,  illustrative  of  the  principal  events  of 
Papal  history,  which  are  executed  in  the  highest  style  of  art,  and  are  often  eloquently 
expressive.  This  work  is  prepared  for  the  people,  and  is  admirably  adapted  to  the  times. 
Its  circulation  would  do  incalculable  good  in  making  known  the  system,  tactics,  errors  and 
dangers  of  Popery,  and  thus  most  effectually  put  us  on  our  guard.  We  trust  its  beautiful 
appearance  will  secure  it  an  introduction  where  its  stirring  appeals  and  thrilling  facts  will 
tell  upon  the  consciences  and  hearts  of  the  people.  The  mechanical  execution  of  this  work 
is  a  credit  to  American  book-making. 

From  the  Olive  Branch  (Boston.) 

This  is  a  magnificent  volume  of  nearly  700  pages,  octavo,  well  fulfilling  the  promises  of 
its  title  page.  Its  engravings  are  of  thrilling  interest  as  are  its  descriptions.  We  give  an 
extended  extract,  commencing  with  page  510,  which  will  show  something  of  its  character. 

From  the  Knickerbocker  Magazine. 

This  volume  is  deemed  to  be  a  complete  and  comprehensive  history  of  Romanism, 
through  the  whole  period  of  its  existence  ;  presenting,  in  chronological  order,  the  origin 
and  progress  of  its  doctrines  and  ceremonies,  the  biographies  of  its  popes,  the  proceedings 
and  decrees  of  its  celebrated  councils,  with  the  details  of  its  rule  over  monarchs  and  states 
in  the  days  of  its  glory,  etc.,  etc.  In  its  typographical  and  pictorial  features,  the  volume 
is  a  superb  one.  It  is  well  printed,  upon  good  paper  ;  the  engravings  are  large,  numerous 
and  exceedingly  well  executed  ;  and  the  -binding  is  beautifully  illustrated  :  in  short,  it 
reflects  the  highest  credit  upon  the  liberality  and  care  of  the  publisher.     It  is  our  belief 

THAT    TEN  THOUSAND    COPIES    WILL    BE    SOLD    IN    LESS    THAN    SIX    MONTHS. 

OPINIONS  OF  THE  SECULAR  PRESS. 

From  the  Providence  Transcript. 

The  Rev.  Mr.  Dowling,  formerly  of  this  city,  has  with  great  labor  devoted  himself  to 
the  preparation  of  the  work  whose  title  we  have  placed  at  the  head  of  this  article.  To 
those  who  are  studying  the  character  of  the  Romish  Church,  this  book  will  be  found  of 
great  assistance.  It  has  received  the  high  praise  in  the  August  number  of  the  Knicker- 
bocker, with  which  we  conclude  this  article.  (The  Transcript  then  copies  and  endorses 
the  foregoing  article  from  the  Knickerbocker.) 

From  the  New  York  American  Republican. 

This  important  work  is  now  before  the  public.  It  commences  with  the  earliest  corrup- 
tions of  Popery,  and  traces  them  to  the  present  time.  It  is  most  admirably  arranged  with 
chronological  and  analytical  tables,  and  also  a  glossary.  Altogether,  this  is  the  most  impor- 
tant work  for  the  use  of  Jumr ':■.<,  for  tlie  rising  generation,  and  the  present  day  mul  age*, 
which  haa  appeared.  It  appears  also,  at  the  very  time  the  public  mind  is  agitated  on  the 
subject  of  the  Romish  Church  ;  when  the  millions  are  aroused  to  a  candid  inquiry  into  the 
nature  and  designs  of  Roman  emissaries  in  America.  "  By  their  fruits  ye  shall  know 
them"  is  a  golden  saying,  and  the  author,  in  this  case,  has  carefully  piled  the  evidence 
mountains  high. 

We  find  in  its  statistics  that  there  are  at  present  over  one  hundred  and  sixty  millions  of 
Catholics  in  the   world.     More  than  one   million  of  whom  are  in  the  United  States,  and 


DOWLING  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

they  have  doubled  in  the  last  ten  years  ;  having  at  this  time  an  annual  accession  of  one 
hundred  thousand  Catholic  immigrants.  Also  that  the  increase  of  the  population  of  the 
United  States  for  every  ten  years  is  only  about  thirty-four  per  cent.  The  learned  author, 
after  wading  through  the  above  and  many  other  facts  of  equally  a  startling  nature,  justly 
concludes  that  there  can  be  no  doubt  that  the  Pope  and  his  adherents  have  formed  the  de- 
liberate design  of  obtaining  the  ascendancy  in  this  country."  Hereafter  we  shall  notice 
this  invaluable  book  farther,  and  most  heartily  do  we  commend  it  to  all  who  want  to  un- 
derstand the  Catholic  question. 

From  the  New  York  Commercial  Advertiser. 

A  large  and  handsome  octavo  of  more  than  650  pages.  Its  author,  the  Rev.  John 
Dowling,  is  pastor  of  the  "  Berean  Church"  in  this  city  ;  and  the  work  is  a  monument  at 
once  of  his  industry  and  of  his  hostility  to  Romanism.  To  describe  its  character  in  brief, 
it  may  be  said  to  constitute  a  well  filled  and  complete  armory  of  weapons  for  use  in 
anti-Papal  warfare;  if  any  man  has  a  desire  to  do  battle,  either  with  tongue  or  pen, 
against  the  Church  of  Rome,  its  ecclesiastical  policy  and  the  practices  of  its  clergy,  ho 
may  here  find  material  in  every  variety  and  in  great  abundance. 

From  the  New  York  Journal  of  Commerce. 

The  period  embraced  in  this  interesting  history  was  replete  with  the  grossest  corruptions 
of  theRomish  Church  ;  and  they  are  here  faithfully  presented,  in  all  their  revolting  details. 
to  the  abhorrence  of  mankind.  The  volume  contains  about  seven  hundred  pages,  verv 
beautifully  printed  and  bound,  and  filled  with  pictorial  illustrations  of  a  creditable  character. 

In  the  preparation  of  his  work,  Mr.  Dowling  has  carefully  avoided  every  occasion  of 
offence  to  Protestant  Christians,  of  whatever  denomination. 

From  the  American  Advocate  (Philadelphia). 

To  fulfil  the  desires  of  such  as  wish  to  become  acquainted  vvitn  Romanism,  and  to  enable 
them  to  put  themselves  in  possession  of  all  the  facts  and  arguments,  sustained  by  appeal  to 
authentic  documents,  which  are  necessary  to  enable  each  one  for  himself  to  form  an 
enlightened  opinion  on  this  important  question,  we  do  not  know  a  work  that  we  can  more 
cheerfully  recommend  to  our  readers  than  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism. 

From  the  Troy  Daily  Whig. 

We  alluded  a  few  days  ago  to  this  then  forthcoming  work,  a  few  of  the  proof  sheets  of 
which  had  been  submitted  to  us.  The  complete  volume  is  now  before  us — rich  in  authentic 
historical  details  and  pictorial  illustrations.  The  book  in  its  embellishments  is  a  credit  to  the 
American  art.  The  engraving,  typography  and  binding  are  all  beautiful,  though  the  intrinsic 
value  of  the  work  scarcely  needed  such  accessories  to  make  it  popular. 

From  the  St.  Joseph's  County  Republican  (Michigan.) 

The  work  contains  nearly  700  pages,  and  fifty  highly  finished  engravings.  It  presents 
Popery  in  its  true  light,  in  all  its  hideous  deformity,  and  unparalleled  barbarity  and  should  be 
circulated  extensively  in  the  Western  Stales,  particularly  in  the  valley  of  the  Mississippi. 

From  the  New  York  Courier  and  Enquirer. 

We  give  the  entire  title  page  of  this  splendid  volume  of  nearly  seven  hundred  pages,  as 
indicating  its  scope  and  object  with  sufficient  distinctness.  There  can  be  no  doubt  that  the 
writer  has  embodied  in  convenient  form  more  matter  in  this  work  than  can  be  elsewhere 
found  in  a  single  volume — more  indeed  than  is  accessible  without  great  difficulty  and  ex- 
pense, to  the  general  reader,  or  indeed  to  the  professional  scholar. 

The  author  is  a  strict  Protestant,  and  it  is  natural  to  suppose  that  his  work  is  in  some 
degree  tinged  with  the  feelings  and  opinions  entertained  by  such  a  man  on  a  subject  of 
the  kind,  but  it  seems  to  us  that  he   has  aimed  at  fairness  and  impartiality. 

The  pictorial  illustrations  are  very  numerous  and  the  most  of  them  very  good.  The  en- 
tire work,  indeed,  is  got  up  in  a  style  which  does  great  credit  to  the  publisher,  and  must, 
have  been  done  at  a  very  large  expense,  as  paper,  type,  binding  and  embellishments  are 
superb.  We  do  not  doubt  that  the  spirit,  enterprise  and  liberality  exhibited  by  Mr.  Walker 
will  be  properly  remunerated,  for  the  author  tells  us  that  he  has  avoided  all  matters  of 
controversy  between  the  different  denominations  of  Protestants  and  Christians,  and  we 
believe  he  has  done  so.  Protestant  Christianity  is  under  obligations,  therefore, 

TO    SUSTAIN  THE   PUBLICATION    IN    MASS.

MISCELLANEOUS. 

The  following  are  selected  from  a  large  number  of  Notices  received  since  the 
preceding  were  in  type  : — 

From  the  Rev.  Doctors  Eaton  and  Maginnis,  Professors  in  Hamilton  Literary  and 

Theological  Seminary. 

Hamilton  Lit.  and  Theo.  Seminary,  Nov.  10th,  1845. 

I  have  with  some  care  looked  through  "  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism,"  and  am  pre- 
pared to  express  a  decidedly  favorable  opinion  of  its  merits.  No  facts  in  history  are  better 
authenticated  than  those  Mr.  Dowling  has  collected,  and  arranged  in  this  volume,  to  illus- 
trate the  principles  and  spirit  of  Romanism.  His  authorities  are  unquestionable  and  may 
be  confidently  relied  on  ;  and  the  whole  work,  with  its  various  illustrations  faithful  as  they 
are  to  fact  and  truth,  gives  a  just  view  of  the  prominent  and  characteristic  features  of  that 
giant  form  of  spiritual  wickedness  and  despotism  so  graphically  delineated  by  the  pen  of 
inspiration,  and  so  frightfully  developed  in  the  history  of  the  Romish  hierarchy.  In  the 
present  day  when  Romanism  under  various  guises  is  making  such  extraordinary  efforts  to 
regain  its  lost  power,  and  extend  its  dominion  over  the  nations  of  the  earth  and  especially 
over  our  own  favored  land,  its  past  history  should  be  familiarly  known  and  well  understood. 
It  claims  to  be  infallible  and  unchangeable,  and  its  history  clearly  shows  that  however  it 
may  vary  its  outward  aspect  to  suit  the  circumstances  and  temper  of  the  times,  in  its  prin- 
ciples and  spirit  it  has  truly  remained  unchanged,  and  from  age  to  age  amid  all  the  changes 
and  vicissitudes  of  the  world's  history,  it  has  from  the  time  of  its  first  casting  its  dark  and 
ominous  shadow  upon  Christendom  down  to  the  present  day  preserved  its  fearful  identity. 
I  am  happy  therefore  to  learn  that  Mr.  Dowling's  authentic  history  is  meeting  with  a  rapid 
sale  and  an  extensive  circulation.  The  Protestant  public  owe  him  many  thanks  for  his 
work,  which  possesses  a  high  value,  and  must  be  one  of  standard  reference  ; — an  armory 
whence  to  draw  weapons  to  battle  with  the  "  Beast,"  and  in  my  opinion  a  fearful  battle  is 
approaching.  Geo.  W.  Eaton,  Professor  of  Civil  and  Ecclesiastical  History, 

Hamilton  Literary  and  Theological  Seminary. 

I  have  examined  the  Rev.  Mr.  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism  and  fully  concur  in  the 
above  recommendation  of  Professor  Eaton.  J.  S.  Maginnis, 

Professor  of  Biblical  Theology. 

From  the  Millcnial  Harbinger,  published  at  Bethany,  Virginia,  and  edited  by  the  celebrated 
Alexander  Campbell,  the  able  and  learned  antagonist  of  Robert  Owen  on  Infidelity,  and 
of  Bishop  Purcell  on  Popery. 

In  this  work  are  abridged  views  of  Romanism  in  all  its  prominent  characteristics  ;  an 
immense  assemblage  of  facts  and  documents  connected  with  the  development  and  history 
of  this  great  mammoth  institution  of  error  and  iniquity.  Popery  is  developed  in  this  treatise 
from  its  birth  to  the  present  hour.  Its  various  and  numerous  errors  are  traced  to  their 
origin.  We  have  in  it  Popery  in  its  origin,  in  its  glory,  in  its  decline,  and  in  its  present 
preternatural  efforts  to  save  itself  from  ruin.  The  world's  midnight,  from  A.  D.  800  to 
Gregory  VII.,  1083,  is  portrayed.  Popery  in  England — the  Waldenses — the  Albigenses — 
The  Protestant  Reformation — the  Inquisition — the  Council  of  Trent — with  a  vivid  account 
of  the  great  reformers  and  opponents  of  the  advances  of  this  Man  of  Sin,  together  with  the 
present  condition  of  Popery  in  Europe  and  America,  constitute  the  prominent  topics  in  this 
interesting  and  elegant  volume. 

We  desire  for  this  book  a  very  large  circulation.  It  is  very  opportune  just  at  this  crisis. 
Everything  civil,  moral,  religious — all  our  birthrights  are  assailed  by  every  advance  of  this 
mammoth  superstition  in  our  beloved  and  happy  land — an  idolatry  and  a  superstition  which 
have  spread  darkness,  error,  delusion  and  misery  over  the  fairest  portion  of  this  earth,  pre- 
vented the  conversion  of  the  world,  and  built  its  fortunes  by  every  species  of  violence — war, 
perfidy,  and  persecution,  upon  the  ruins  of  man's  best  and  dearest  rights  of  personal  liberty, 
life,  and  happiness.  It  is  only  necessary  to  enlighten  this  community  in  the  true  spirit  and 
character  of  the  Papal  system,  to  secure  them  against  its  insidious  attacks  upon  everything 
we  as  a  people  hold  dear  and  sacred.  Let  every  one  who  buvs  this  work  communicate 
freely  to  all  around  him  the  developments  which  it  reveals,  and  the  facts  which 
it  p.ecoeds."  Alexander  Campbell. 

From  the  Christian  Secretary,  Hartford. 

Dowling's  History  of  Romanism. — Having  given  this  book  a  pretty  thorough  exami- 
nation, we  are  prepared  to  speak  of  he  merits.     The  volume  contains  nearly  seven  hundred 


DOWLING  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

pages  and  is  divided  into  nine  books,  as  follows  :  Popery  in  Embryo, — Popery  at  its  Birth, — 
Popery  advancing, — Popery  in  its  glory, — Popery  the  World's  Despot, — Popery  on  a  Tot- 
tering Throne, — Popery  at  Trent, — Popery  drunk  with  the  Blood  of  the  Saints, — Popery 
in  its  Dotage. 

The  author  commences  with  the  earliest  corruptions  of  Christianity,  and  traces  Popery 
from  its  rise  to  its  full  development  in  606  under  Pope  Boniface,  and  from  thence  down  to 
the  present  time.  We  do  not  see  how  Mr.  Dowling,  or  any  other  author,  could  condense 
a  History  of  Romanism,  fruitful  as  it  is  of  deeds  of  darkness  and  of  blood,  into  a  smaller 
compass,  and  still  exhibit  all  its  odious  features  in  so  striking  a  light. 

Mr.  Dowling  has  done  good  service  to  the  cause  of  evangelical  truth  in  preparing  this 
volume  for  the  press, — a  task  that  must  have  cost  him  immense  labor  and  research — and 

WE  SHOULD  BE  GLAD  TO  HAVE  A  COPY  OF  IT  PLACED  IN  THE  HANDS  OF  EVERY  FAMILY  IN  THE 

country,  for  it  would  serve  as  an  excellent  barrier  against  the  wiles  of  Romanism,  with 
which  we  are  already  too  plentifully  supplied. 

From  the  American  Review  {Quarterly),  New  York. 

"This  book,  in  a  compass  of  nearly  700  pages,  presents  a  fuller  account  of  the  Rise  and 
Progress — the  picturesque  and  terrible  history — of  the  ghostly  power  of  Rome,  than  has 
yet  been  given  to  Protestant  readers  in  this  country.  *  *  *  *  Of  her  general 
Persecutions,  her  Superstitions,  her  gorgeous  Ceremonials,  it  is  the  most  complete  and 
vivid  memorial  ive  have  seen.  It  is  also  embellished  with  a  great  number  of  engravings, 
executed  with  much  effect.  Four  editions,  we  understand,  have  been  sold,  and  the  work 
will  undoubtedly  find  an  entrance  into  many  thousand  families." 

From  the  Parlor  Magazine,  New  York. 

"  Dowling's  History  of  Romanism. — The  most  elegant  book  in  point  of  typographical 
and  mechanical  beauty  which  the  last  month  has  laid  on  our  table,  is  the  History  of  Roman- 
ism, from  the  earliest  corruptions  of  Christianity  to  the  present  times,  with  full  chronologi- 
cal tables,  analytical  and  alphabetical  indexes  and  glossary  ;  illustrated  by  numerous  accu- 
rate and  highly  finished  engravings  of  its  ceremonies,  superstitions,  persecutions,  and  his- 
torical incidents.  By  Rev.  John  Dowling,  A.M.  It  is  published  by  Edward  Walker,  who 
is  entitled  to  great  commendation  for  the  style  in  which  it  is  brought  out.  The  want  of 
such  a  work  as  this  has  been  often  felt ;  for  while  we  were  abundantly  supplied  with  books 
on  Popery,  and  there  are  also  very  elaborate  and  voluminous  histories  of  Romanism,  there 
has  been  no  work  of  convenient  size,  of  sufficient  comprehensiveness,  and  so  arranged  as 
to  furnish  the  general  reader  with  a  concise,  lucid  and  graphic  view  of  the  greatest  system 
of  error  and  imposture  which  the  world  ever  saw.  Mr.  Dowling  has  availed  himself  freely 
of  the  valuable  labors  of  others,  and  has  made  a  book  which  we  trust  will  be  widely  circu- 
lated and  greatly  useful  in  this  land  and  day  of  ours.  We  must  add  that  the  engravings  in 
this  volume  are  among  the  best  specimens  of  wood  engraving  we  ever  saw  ;  they  are 
by  Lossing." 

From  the  Montreal  Register,  edited  by  the  Rev.  Dr.  Cramp,  President  of  the  Baptist  Col- 
lege at  Montreal,  and  Author  of  the  History  of  the  Council  of  Trent,  Reformation  in 
Europe,  dfc. 

We  close  this  list  of  testimonials  with  the  following  partly  because  of  the  high  character 
of  the  writer  as  a  faithful  Protestant  historian,  and  partly  on  account  of  the  brief  but  com- 
prehensive account  of  the  contents  of  each  of  the  nine  books  of  the  "  History  of  Romanism." 

"We  have  just  received  a  copy  of  this  important  work,  but  have  not  been  able  to  give  it 
the  attention  which  it  demands.  A  cursory  inspection,  however,  has  convinced  us  that  it 
possesses  merits  of  a  high  order.  We  are  pleased  to  see  that  Mr.  Dowling  furnishes 
ample  references  to  his  authorities.  It  is  our  intention  to  furnish  a  full  review  in  our  next 
number.  Meanwhile  we  beg  to  assure  our  friends  in  Montreal,  that  they  may  give  their 
names  to  the  Agent  without  hesitation.  Three  dollars  will  be  well  laid  out  in  buying  a 
copy  of  this  book." 

From  the  next  number  of  the  same  paper. 

»"  In  proceeding  to  give  an  account  of  this  work,  agreeably  to  our  promise,  we  will  tran- 
scribe the  titles  of  the  various  books  into  which  it  is  divided,  and  state  their  contents. 

Book  I.  Popery  in  embryo ;  from  the  earliest  corruptions  of  Christianity  to  the  Papal 
Sapremacy,  A.  D.,  606. — Having  explained  the  spiritual  nature  and  objects  of  Christianity, 
Mr.  Dowling  shows  that  its  corruptions  were  foreseen  and  predicted,  even  in  apostolic 


LITERARY    NOTICES    OF    DOWLING  S    HISTORY    OF    ROMANISM. 

times.     lie  then  describes  the  changes  that  took  place  soon  after  the  accession  of  Con- 
stantine,  and  traces  the  usurpation  of  the  Bishop  of  Rome  from  Victor  to  Boniface. 

Book  II.  Popery  at  its  birth,  A.  D.,  COG.  Its  doctrinal  and  ritual  character  at  this 
epoch. — The  origin  of  Romish  errors  is  the  subject  of  the  second  book.  Seventy  pages  are 
devoted  to  it.  Conclusive  evidence  is  furnished  in  support  of  the  assertion,  that  the  celibacy 
of  the  clergy,  the  Worship  of  the  Virgin  Mary,  monkery,  and  the  adoration  of  saints  and 
relics,  are  not  scriptural  practices,  but  novelties  introduced  by  carnally-minded  men.  In 
the  sixth  chapter,  the  resemblance  between  Pagan  and  Papal  ceremonies  is  strikingly  ex- 
hibited in  a  judicious  abridgement  of  Dr.  Cony  ers  Middleton's  celebrated  work  on  that  subject. 

Book  III.  Popery  advancing ;  from  the  establishment  of  the  spiritual  supremacy,  A.  D. 
606,  to  the  Pope's  temporal  sovereignty,  A.  D.  756,  and  to  the  crowning  of  the  Emperor 
Charlemagne,  A.  D.  800. — The  progress  of  the  spiritual  despotism  is  still  further  traced  to 
the  time  when  the  Romish  bishop  became  a  temporal  sovereign,  and  the  pretended  succes- 
sor of  him  who  said,  t:  Silver  and  gold  have  I  none,"  assumed  the  tiara,  and  received  taxes 
and  tribute.  In  this  period  image-worship  was  invented,  a  long  controversy  followed,  but 
the  abomination  ultimately  prevailed. 

Book  IV.  Popery  in  its  glory.  The  world's  midnight.  From  the  coronation  of  Charle- 
magne, A.  D.  800,  to  the  beginning  of  the  Pontificate  of  Pope  Hildebrand,  or  Gregory  the 
Seventh.  A.  D.  1073. — This  book  contains  an  affecting  account  of  the  vile  forgeries,  "lying 
wonders,"  gross  impositions,  and  ridiculous  rites,  by  which  the  pretensions  of  the  "  Man  of 
Sin  "  were  sustained,  and  his  influence  extended.  The  exploits  of  the  English  saint,  Dun- 
Btaa,  who  pinched  the  devil's  nose  with  a  pair  of  red-hot  tongs,  and  achieved  other  won- 
ders, the  monkish  narrations  of  which  are  deeply  tinged  with  blasphemy,  occupy  a  con- 
spicuous place  here. 

Book  V.  Popery,  the  world's  despot  :  from  the  accession  of  Pope  Gregory  VII.,  A.  D. 
1073,  to  the  death  of  Boniface  VIII.,  A.  D.  1303. — A  wide  field  is  before  the  historian  in 
this  division  of  his  work.  The  lives  of  those  paragons  of  pride  and  cruelty,  Popes 
Gregory  VII.,  Innocent  III.,  and  Boniface  VIII.  ; — the  story  of  the  crusades; — the  pranks 
of  Thomas  a  Becket  ; — the  unparalleled  sufferings  of  the  Albigenses  and  Wa'denses,  in- 
flicted by  papal  command  ; — the  establishment  of  the  mendicant  orders  ; — the  wars  of  the 
Guelphs  and  Ghibelines  ; — together  with  numerous  additions  to  the  burdensome  ritual  of 
the  enthralled  church,  furnish  topics  which  the  author  has  treated  with  his  accustomed 
skill. 

Book  VI.     Popery  on  a  tottering  throne  ;  from  the  deattf  of  Boniface  VIII.,  A  .  D.  1303. 
to  the  commencement  of  the  Council  of  Trent,  A.  D.  1545. — The  throne  seemed  to  totter 
a  this  period,  and  some  expected  to  see  it  fall.     By  the  residence  of  the  Popes  at 
qod,  and  the  Great  Western  schism,  anti-Christian  influence  was  considerably  weak- 
ened.    The  efforts  of  John  Wickliff,  John  Huss,  and  Jerome  of  Prague,  followed  by  the 
1  of  learning,  inflicted  serious  wouryds  on  the  Papacy.     Luther  and  his  zealous  coad- 
jutors aimed  a  deadly  blow.     Yet  notwithstanding  these  disastrous  events,  Popery  continued 
io  exist  and  even  regained  strength.     To  this,  the  establishment  of  the  order  of  the  JesuiU. 
i.i  1540,  '.  ■  ry  materially  contributed. 

Book  VII.  Popery  at  Trent  :  from  the  opening  session  of  the  Council  of  Trent,  A.  J). 
1545,  to  the  closing  Session,  A.  D.  15f>3. — The  Council  of  Trent  was  an  infamous  chea: 
on  the  world.     This  abridgement  of  its  history  is  a  fair  exposure  of  the  cheat. 

Book  VIII.  Popery  drunk  with  the  blood  of  the  saints.  Persecutions  of  Popery,  to  the. 
revocation  of  the  edict  of  Nantes,  A.  D.  1685. — Harrowing  details  of  persecution — com- 
prising the  history  of  the  English  martyrs,  of  the  Inquisition,  of  the  sufferings  of  the 
Waldcnses,  of  the  massacre  of  St.  Bartholomew,  and  of  the  revocation  of  the  Edkt  of 
Nantes,  are  contained  in  this  book. 

B  iok  IX.  Popery  in  its  dotage  ;  from  the  revocation  of  the  edict  of  Nantes,  A.  D.  1685. 
to  the  present  time,  A.  D.  1815. — The  history  of  the  Jesuits  is  sketched,  and  various  facts 
are  adduced,  showing  that  Popery  is  unchanged.  The  volume  closes  with  a  statement  of 
the  present  position  and  zealous  efforts  of  the  Romish  church.

IMPORTANT  NATIONAL  WORK. 

0 

IN  THE  PRESS,  and  nearly  ready,  a  New  and  Enlarged  Edition  of 

PRESIDENTS'    MESSAGES. 

In  Two  Handsome  Volumes  8vo.     The  whole  collected  from  Official  Documents, 
BY  E.  WILLIAMS,  Esq. 

CONTEXTS. 

1.  The  Messages,  Addresses  and  Speeches  of  the  Presidents  of  the  United  States, 

from  Washington  to  Polk ;  with  a  copious  Index  to  the  same,  of  subjects, 
names,  and  dates. 

2.  An  account  of  the  Inaguration  of  each  President,  and  a  brief  notice  of  the  princi- 

pal political  events  of  his  administration. 

3.  A  Biographical  Sketch  of  each  President. 

4.  Declaration  of  Independence. 

5.  Articles  of  Confederation,  with  a  brief  History  of  the  events  and  circumstances 

which  led  to  the  union  of  the  States  and  the  formation  of  the  Constitution. 

6.  Constitution  of  the  United  States,  with  notes  and  references. 

7.  A  Synopsis  of  the  Constitutions  of  the  several  States. 

8.  Chronological  Table  of  Historical  Events  in  the  United  States. 

9.  Tables  of  Members  of  the  Cabinets  of  the  various  administrations,  Ministers  to 

Foreign  Countries,  and  other  principal  public  officers. 

10.  Statistical  Tables  of  Commerce  and  Population  ; 

11.  With  Portraits  of  the  Presidents. 

12.  The  whole  carefully  collated  from  Congressional  Documents  and  accompanied 

with  an  Analytical  Index.
